reasoning: A military superior ordering subordinates to perform personal manual labor (washing a private vehicle, mowing a lawn) at a private residence is a textbook abuse of authority. No reasonable person would defend this as a legitimate military order; it is clearly personal servitude disguised as duty.

A military commander clearly lacks legal jurisdiction to regulate the private speech of civilian spouses living off-base. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this as an illegitimate extension of military authority over private citizens, regardless of the content of the speech.

A platoon leader demanding warrantless access to personal devices for a 'fishing expedition' regarding attitudes clearly exceeds their command authority. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this as an illegitimate invasion of privacy and a violation of protocol, as search authorization typically requires higher command approval and probable cause.

The injustice is clear because the military authority is attempting to assert legal jurisdiction (UCMJ punishment) over a person who is legally a civilian at the time. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that a commander cannot dictate the personal movements of a reservist who is not on active orders, making the authority's command illegitimate.

The authority is a military junta that seized power via a coup only 48 hours ago, which is the textbook definition of illegitimate authority in a constitutional framework. As explicitly noted in the calibration guidelines, a reasonable person would immediately recognize that a junta lacks the legitimate mandate to govern or enforce martial law.

The authority figure is a General who has explicitly suspended the constitution and is acting against the elected President. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this as a military coup with no legal legitimacy to issue orders, particularly orders to deploy weapons against civilians.

A First Sergeant (NCO) generally lacks the legal authority to authorize searches or demand personal passwords, a power reserved for Commanders or Magistrates based on probable cause. A reasonable person would immediately recognize a warrantless, whimsical demand for private social media credentials by a non-commanding officer as a clear excess of delegated authority.

The military commander clearly lacks jurisdiction to issue binding orders or threaten AWOL status for a reservist who is not on active duty or drill status. A reasonable person would immediately recognize that a military officer cannot dictate the movements of a private citizen working a civilian job.

Ordering a subordinate to perform unpaid personal manual labor (roofing, painting) at a superior's private residence is a textbook abuse of military authority and a violation of regulations. No reasonable person would defend this as a legitimate exercise of military command; it is clearly personal servitude disguised as duty.

A rebel militia that seizes territory by force has no legal or moral standing to abduct and conscript citizens. No reasonable person would recognize the authority of a non-state armed group to detain civilians against their will, making the illegitimacy of their command immediate and obvious.
